mnemonics.title: SCHEDULING CRITERIA
mnemonics.content: C-U-T-W-T-R: CPU Utilization, Underline Throughput, Waiting Time, Turnaround time, Response time. Helps remember all 5 criteria in order.

mnemonics.title: TYPES OF SCHEDULERS
mnemonics.content: L-M-S: Long-term Manages admission, Medium-term Manages swapping, Short-term Manages CPU selection. Remember as LMS in alphabetical order of process lifecycle.

mnemonics.title: TURNAROUND TIME FORMULA
mnemonics.content: TAT = CT - AT. Remember: Complete Time minus Arrival Time equals TAT.

mnemonics.title: WAITING TIME FORMULA
mnemonics.content: WT = TAT - BT. Remember: TAT minus BT equals WT.

mnemonics.title: SCHEDULER TYPES BY FREQUENCY
mnemonics.content: LONG LASTS (low frequency), MEDIUM MIGHT (medium frequency), SHORT SPEEDS (high frequency). Long-term runs rarely, short-term runs every few milliseconds.

acronyms.usage: Non-preemptive scheduling algorithm that serves processes in order of arrival
Algorithm that prioritizes process with smallest next CPU burst
Preemptive time-sharing scheduling using time quantum
The main processor that executes process instructions
Total time from process arrival to completion
Total time process spends in ready queue
Time when process enters the system/ready queue
Time required by process to complete CPU execution
Time from arrival to first CPU allocation

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flow: NEW → Long-term → READY → Short-term → RUNNING → EXIT. Add Medium-term between READY and SUSPENDED for swapping.
Imagine a movie theater queue (READY) where people wait for the single ticket counter (CPU). FCFS is like first-come-first-served line, Priority is like VIP lanes.
Think of context switch like a relay race handoff - runner (process) passes the baton (CPU state) to next runner. The handoff takes time and no one is running during transfer.
Preemptive is like a teacher taking away chalk from one student to give to another. Non-preemptive is like letting a student finish their presentation before anyone else presents.
CPU-bound = marathon runner (long continuous effort), I/O-bound = sprinter with breaks (short burst then wait for I/O)

analogies.analogy: Just like a traffic signal controls which cars go through an intersection, the CPU scheduler controls which process gets CPU time at any moment.
The ready queue is like a waiting room at a doctor's office where patients (processes) wait for their turn to see the doctor (CPU).
Context switch is like when you stop working on one assignment to work on another - you save your progress (state) and open the new assignment (restore state).

analogies.concept: Degree of Multiprogramming as Concurrent Tasks
analogies.analogy: Degree of multiprogramming is like how many browser tabs you have open at once - more tabs mean more tasks in memory but more memory consumption.
Throughput is like a factory's production rate - more units completed per hour means higher throughput. The CPU scheduler tries to maximize this output.
